text: The surgeon performs an ETEP procedure with a right-sided approach, revealing a suprapubic defect. The surgeon's right hand is positioned almost perpendicular to the defect, indicating potential difficulty in suturing.
The surgeon's left hand maneuvers with ample space, approaching the suprapubic defect more parallel compared to the right hand, which faces difficulty due to its perpendicular angle.
The surgeon swaps the instruments between the left and right hands and then rotates the camera to adjust the view.
The surgeon uses the right hand, previously the left hand, to suture the suprapubic defect with improved access and angle, facilitating the closure similar to a laparotomy incision.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to take precise bites of the fascia and hernia sac, demonstrating careful suturing technique.

The surgeon completes suturing the defect, rotating the scope back to its original orientation. The surgical instruments are reassigned, and the surgeon measures the space before laying the mesh into the repaired area.

The surgeon is docked on the patient's right side, addressing a lower abdominal defect. The right linea alba is positioned close to the right hand, which approaches perpendicularly, presenting a challenge for suturing the ipsilateral linea alba.

The surgeon swaps instruments and manually rotates the camera upside down, tapping the touchpad to flip the image. The master controllers are reassigned to control the opposite instruments after the image inversion.
The surgeon uses the right hand to suture both edges of the defect, taking solid bites of the left and right linea alba.

The surgeon inserts the needle perpendicular to the tissue, taking a solid bite to imbricate the midline hernia sac and attenuated fascial tissue. The anterior rectus sheath on the screen's left side is retracted laterally in front of the rectus muscle. Using the inverted image view, the surgeon peels back the rectus muscle to more easily grasp the anterior sheath.
